lenses.guidance: 'I AM WHO I AM' (v 14) — Hebrew ehyeh asher ehyeh, a verbal formula on the root hyh ('to be'). Untensed and unconditioned. The original audience hears self-existence claimed in the same breath as covenant presence (v 12).

The bush burns but is not consumed (v 2) — divine presence sustained without destroying its vessel. Patristic readers saw a type of the incarnation: God in flesh that does not consume the human nature he assumes.

'I have observed... I have heard... I know' (vv 7-9) names the moment covenant memory becomes covenant action. The redemptive arc opened at Gen 15:13-14 is now activated in commission, name, and place (vv 8, 10).

When Jesus says 'before Abraham was, I AM' (Jn 8:58) the bush of v 14 is the citation. John's seven 'I am' sayings structure around this single OT root. The God who speaks in fire is the Christ who speaks in flesh.

Horeb (v 1) is named again at 1 Kgs 19:8 in Elijah's encounter, and the v 14 name echoes through Isaiah's 'I am he' formulas (Isa 41:4, 43:10, 45:5) — a canonical thread across Scripture defining God's self-disclosure.

Moses removes his sandals (v 5) before approaching. Worship begins in posture: physical recognition that the ground God names is not the ground we own. Today, draw near with reverence rather than familiarity.
